one_line_takeaway: Even state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs (including GPT-5) are measurably vulnerable to prompt injection hidden in phishing-site HTML/URLs that is imperceptible to human victims, and while the authors' InjectDefuser defense (context-boundary hardening + allowlist RAG + output validation) sharply cuts attack success on the strongest model (GPT-5: 39.9% to 0.3%), weaker/open-weight models remain substantially exposed (Llama 4 Maverick: 84.7% to 61.7%).
key_finding: HTML-based PI ASR (Table 2): Standard mode -- GPT-5 39.9% (797/2000), Grok 4 Fast 65.1% (1302/2000), Llama 4 Maverick 84.7% (1693/2000), Gemma 3 27B 64.7% (1293/2000). Advanced mode -- GPT-5 dropped to 10.1% (201/2000) but Grok 4 Fast counterintuitively rose to 76.2% (1524/2000); Llama 4 75.6% (1512/2000), Gemma 3 55.4% (1107/2000). InjectDefuser -- GPT-5 0.3% (5/2000, all from malformed-JSON failures), Grok 4 Fast 26.1% (522/2000), Llama 4 61.7% (1233/2000), Gemma 3 36.7% (733/2000). By attack technique, Legitimate Pretexting was most effective (Standard mode: 58.3% GPT-5, 97.3% Grok 4 Fast; Advanced mode up to 99.8% Grok 4 Fast). Invisible attack surfaces (metadata, script/comment, invisible-DOM content) yielded consistently higher ASR than visible surfaces (visible HTML content, embedded-resource images) across all models. URL-only PI (Table 3, 200 samples): GPT-5 Standard 20.0%, Advanced 1.0%, InjectDefuser 0.0%; Grok 4 Fast Standard 33.5%, Advanced 2.0%, InjectDefuser 8.0% (worse than Advanced); Llama 4 Standard 22.0%, Advanced 16.0%, InjectDefuser 7.0%; Gemma 3 0.0% at every mode. On third-party pipelines (Table 4, 40 samples): CRP prediction ASR was GPT-5 20.0%, Grok 4 Fast 7.5%, Llama 4 25.0%, Gemma 3 2.5%; Brand Extraction ASR was GPT-5 12.5%, Grok 4 Fast 5.0%, Llama 4 2.5%, Gemma 3 2.5% -- notably GPT-5 was the most vulnerable model for brand extraction, which the authors attribute to more capable models attending more closely to embedded text and thus executing subtler injected instructions. InjectDefuser's measured overhead on GPT-5 averaged 14.65s total processing time per site (3.08s of that attributable to InjectDefuser's own non-LLM processing) and added a minimum of 573 input tokens (~$0.00072 per call).
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic study of prompt injection (PI) against multimodal LLM-based phishing-site detectors. It contributes: (1) a two-axis taxonomy of PI attacks for this setting -- five main Attack Techniques (Legitimate Pretexting, Role Hijacking, Safety Policy Triggering, Tool/Function Hijacking, Content Flooding/Distraction) plus two auxiliary techniques (Stealth Encoding, Parser Boundary Confusion), crossed with six Attack Surfaces (HTML Metadata, Script and Comment, HTML Visible Content, HTML Invisible Content, Embedded Resources, URL Structure); (2) an empirical attack-success-rate (ASR) evaluation of four LLMs (GPT-5, Grok 4 Fast Non-Reasoning, Llama 4 Maverick, Gemma 3 27B) across three prompting modes (Standard, Advanced -- an extension of the authors' own prior ChatPhishDetector system [13], and their new InjectDefuser defense) on HTML-based PI, URL-only PI, and PI against three pre-existing third-party phishing-detection pipelines (PhishLLM, KnowPhish, PhishAgent); and (3) InjectDefuser itself, a defense framework combining UUID-tagged context-boundary prompt hardening, an Allowlist RAG (brand extraction via a small LLM + Chroma vector-DB similarity search against legitimate domains), and output validation (JSON schema/type/range checks).
threat_model: Adversary controls the entire phishing site (URL, HTML, CSS, JS, images) but is constrained to preserve the site's credential-harvesting functionality and to remain imperceptible/non-suspicious to the human victim while still being processed by the LLM detector -- the paper calls this 'perceptual asymmetry.' The targeted system is an automated LLM-based classifier that ingests HTML, a rendered screenshot, and the URL and outputs a phishing/not-phishing verdict in JSON. No white-box access to the system prompt is assumed (the 25 PI messages avoid referencing specific field names like is_phishing).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) they searched urlscan.io for real phishing pages containing prompt-injection keywords and found no confirmed in-the-wild PI instances, so real-world deployment/prevalence of this attack is unverified; (2) the evaluation measures whether the LLM ingests hidden instructions, not actual human-perceptibility -- no user study was run to confirm the injected text is truly imperceptible to victims; (3) attack techniques were tested in isolation per sample rather than combined, though real adversaries could stack multiple techniques/surfaces; (4) InjectDefuser is an add-on prompt/RAG/validation layer that does not modify the underlying model or eliminate inherent PI susceptibility -- the authors note fine-tuning/alignment-based defenses would be more fundamental; (5) Allowlist RAG effectiveness depends on the coverage/freshness of the brand-domain database, an ongoing maintenance burden, and the paper's own RAG database was built only from the 10 evaluated brands rather than a broad list like Tranco; (6) because the defense layers are interdependent, the authors did not run a component-wise ablation of InjectDefuser, evaluating only end-to-end robustness; (7) some non-zero ASR persisted for every model even under InjectDefuser (e.g., GPT-5 1.0% on Content Flooding, Llama 4 61.7% overall on HTML-based PI), and a fail-closed policy for LLM refusals/errors was proposed but not validated as safe against false positives.

datasets_used: 2,000-sample HTML-based PI dataset: 10 most-targeted brands (Q2 2025 APWG list: Adobe, Amazon, Apple, Booking, Facebook, Google, LinkedIn, Microsoft, Spotify, WhatsApp) x 25 PI messages (Table 5, appendix) x 8 HTML insertion locations (Table 1), built by the authors on synthetic login-page templates with randomized nonsense domains
200-sample URL-only PI dataset: 5 message types x 4 URL components (subdomain, path, query, fragment) x 10 brands, combined with unmodified base-HTML screenshots
40-sample third-party-pipeline dataset: 4 PI types x 10 brands, near-background-colored HTML Visible Content injections, used against reimplemented PhishLLM/KnowPhish/PhishAgent components
quantitative_results: HTML-based PI ASR, Standard mode: GPT-5 39.9% (797/2000), Grok 4 Fast 65.1% (1302/2000), Llama 4 Maverick 84.7% (1693/2000), Gemma 3 27B 64.7% (1293/2000)
HTML-based PI ASR, InjectDefuser mode: GPT-5 0.3% (5/2000), Grok 4 Fast 26.1% (522/2000), Llama 4 61.7% (1233/2000), Gemma 3 36.7% (733/2000)
URL-only PI ASR (200 samples): GPT-5 20.0% -> 1.0% -> 0.0% (Standard/Advanced/InjectDefuser); Grok 4 Fast 33.5% -> 2.0% -> 8.0%; Llama 4 22.0% -> 16.0% -> 7.0%; Gemma 3 0.0% across all modes
Existing-pipeline ASR (40 samples): CRP prediction -- GPT-5 20.0%, Grok 4 Fast 7.5%, Llama 4 25.0%, Gemma 3 2.5%; Brand Extraction -- GPT-5 12.5%, Grok 4 Fast 5.0%, Llama 4 2.5%, Gemma 3 2.5%
InjectDefuser overhead on GPT-5: 14.65s average total processing time per site, 3.08s attributable to InjectDefuser's own processing, minimum +573 input tokens (~$0.00072/call)
Explicit PI-recognition mentions in model rationale under InjectDefuser: GPT-5 871 cases, Grok 4 Fast 734, Gemma 3 99, Llama 4 only 3
Cross-brand ASR variation was small: range of 11-17pp (max 22pp) across the 10 brands, Cramer's V approx 0.10-0.15
baselines_compared: Standard mode (bare 'Is this a phishing site?' prompt)
Advanced mode (extension of the authors' own prior system, ChatPhishDetector [13])
PhishLLM [11] -- reimplemented screenshot-only CRP binary classifier
KnowPhish [14] -- reimplemented for CRP prediction and Brand Extraction (no public implementation available)
PhishAgent [12] -- reimplemented screenshot-to-text Brand Extraction (no public implementation available)
